exercises.prompt: Dispatch wants January's daily volume smoothed to spot unusual days. First aggregate the full requests table into one row per created_date with reqs = COUNT(*). Then compute rolling_7d = ROUND(AVG(reqs) OVER (ORDER BY created_date ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW), 2) — the current day plus the six before it. Finally keep only days in January 2026 (created_date from 2026-01-01 through 2026-01-31) and return dt (the date), reqs, rolling_7d, and day_type (CASE: 'spike' when reqs are greater than rolling_7d * 1.25, 'lull' when reqs are less than rolling_7d * 0.75, otherwise 'normal'). Order by dt ascending. Build the rolling average over the whole series in a CTE before filtering to January, so the first shown days still have a full seven-day window behind them.
exercises.referenceSql: WITH daily AS (SELECT created_date AS dt, COUNT(*) AS reqs FROM requests GROUP BY created_date), rolled AS (SELECT dt, reqs, ROUND(AVG(reqs) OVER (ORDER BY dt ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW), 2) AS rolling_7d FROM daily) SELECT dt, reqs, rolling_7d, CASE WHEN reqs > rolling_7d * 1.25 THEN 'spike' WHEN reqs < rolling_7d * 0.75 THEN 'lull' ELSE 'normal' END AS day_type FROM rolled WHERE dt >= DATE '2026-01-01' AND dt <= DATE '2026-01-31' ORDER BY dt
hints: The trap is that WHERE runs before the window, so if you filter to January first, the early January days lose the six December days behind them. Build a daily CTE (one row per created_date with COUNT(*)) over the whole dataset, a rolled CTE that adds the ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW average across that full series, and only then filter to January in the outer query and label each day with CASE.

WITH daily AS (SELECT created_date AS dt, COUNT(*) AS reqs FROM requests GROUP BY created_date), rolled AS (SELECT dt, reqs, ROUND(AVG(reqs) OVER (ORDER BY dt ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW), 2) AS rolling_7d FROM daily) SELECT dt, reqs, rolling_7d, CASE WHEN reqs > rolling_7d * 1.25 THEN 'spike' WHEN reqs < rolling_7d * 0.75 THEN 'lull' ELSE 'normal' END AS day_type FROM rolled WHERE dt >= DATE '2026-01-01' AND dt <= DATE '2026-01-31' ORDER BY dt.

```sql
WITH daily AS (SELECT created_date AS dt, COUNT(*) AS reqs FROM requests GROUP BY created_date), rolled AS (SELECT dt, reqs, ROUND(AVG(reqs) OVER (ORDER BY dt ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW), 2) AS rolling_7d FROM daily) SELECT dt, reqs, rolling_7d, CASE WHEN reqs > rolling_7d * 1.25 THEN 'spike' WHEN reqs < rolling_7d * 0.75 THEN 'lull' ELSE 'normal' END AS day_type FROM rolled WHERE dt >= DATE '2026-01-01' AND dt <= DATE '2026-01-31' ORDER BY dt
```

exercises.prompt: Which departments saw their caseload rise from May to June 2026? Aggregate requests into one row per (department, month) with reqs = COUNT(*), bucketing the month as CAST(DATE_TRUNC('month', created_date) AS DATE). For each department attach prev_reqs = the previous month's count using LAG partitioned by department and ordered by month. Keep only the June 2026 row (month = 2026-06-01) for departments that have a previous-month value, and return department, mon (the month), reqs, prev_reqs, pct_change = ROUND(100.0 * (reqs - prev_reqs) / prev_reqs, 1), and trend (CASE: 'rising' when reqs are greater than prev_reqs, 'falling' when less, otherwise 'flat'). Order by pct_change descending, then department ascending.
exercises.referenceSql: WITH monthly AS (SELECT department, CAST(DATE_TRUNC('month', created_date) AS DATE) AS mon, COUNT(*) AS reqs FROM requests GROUP BY department, mon), with_prev AS (SELECT department, mon, reqs, LAG(reqs) OVER (PARTITION BY department ORDER BY mon) AS prev_reqs FROM monthly) SELECT department, mon, reqs, prev_reqs, ROUND(100.0 * (reqs - prev_reqs) / prev_reqs, 1) AS pct_change, CASE WHEN reqs > prev_reqs THEN 'rising' WHEN reqs < prev_reqs THEN 'falling' ELSE 'flat' END AS trend FROM with_prev WHERE mon = DATE '2026-06-01' AND prev_reqs IS NOT NULL ORDER BY pct_change DESC, department
hints: Bucket to monthly counts per department first, then reach back one month with LAG. A monthly CTE groups requests by department and DATE_TRUNC('month', created_date) cast to DATE. A with_prev CTE adds LAG(reqs) OVER (PARTITION BY department ORDER BY mon) so June sees May. The outer query filters to the June 2026 row (and requires prev_reqs to exist), computes the rounded percent change, and labels the trend with CASE.

WITH monthly AS (SELECT department, CAST(DATE_TRUNC('month', created_date) AS DATE) AS mon, COUNT(*) AS reqs FROM requests GROUP BY department, mon), with_prev AS (SELECT department, mon, reqs, LAG(reqs) OVER (PARTITION BY department ORDER BY mon) AS prev_reqs FROM monthly) SELECT department, mon, reqs, prev_reqs, ROUND(100.0 * (reqs - prev_reqs) / prev_reqs, 1) AS pct_change, CASE WHEN reqs > prev_reqs THEN 'rising' WHEN reqs < prev_reqs THEN 'falling' ELSE 'flat' END AS trend FROM with_prev WHERE mon = DATE '2026-06-01' AND prev_reqs IS NOT NULL ORDER BY pct_change DESC, department.

```sql
WITH monthly AS (SELECT department, CAST(DATE_TRUNC('month', created_date) AS DATE) AS mon, COUNT(*) AS reqs FROM requests GROUP BY department, mon), with_prev AS (SELECT department, mon, reqs, LAG(reqs) OVER (PARTITION BY department ORDER BY mon) AS prev_reqs FROM monthly) SELECT department, mon, reqs, prev_reqs, ROUND(100.0 * (reqs - prev_reqs) / prev_reqs, 1) AS pct_change, CASE WHEN reqs > prev_reqs THEN 'rising' WHEN reqs < prev_reqs THEN 'falling' ELSE 'flat' END AS trend FROM with_prev WHERE mon = DATE '2026-06-01' AND prev_reqs IS NOT NULL ORDER BY pct_change DESC, department
```

exercises.prompt: Staffing wants departments ranked by total workload. Aggregate the full requests table to one row per department with total_reqs = COUNT(*) and distinct_types = COUNT(DISTINCT service_request_type). Return department, total_reqs, distinct_types, load_rank = RANK() OVER (ORDER BY total_reqs DESC) with tied departments sharing a rank, and load_tier (CASE: 'critical' when total_reqs are at least 60000, 'heavy' when at least 30000, 'moderate' when at least 5000, otherwise 'light'). Order by total_reqs descending, then department ascending.
exercises.referenceSql: WITH dept_totals AS (SELECT department, COUNT(*) AS total_reqs, COUNT(DISTINCT service_request_type) AS distinct_types FROM requests GROUP BY department) SELECT department, total_reqs, distinct_types, RANK() OVER (ORDER BY total_reqs DESC) AS load_rank, CASE WHEN total_reqs >= 60000 THEN 'critical' WHEN total_reqs >= 30000 THEN 'heavy' WHEN total_reqs >= 5000 THEN 'moderate' ELSE 'light' END AS load_tier FROM dept_totals ORDER BY total_reqs DESC, department
hints: A single CTE (dept_totals) groups requests by department for total_reqs = COUNT(*) and distinct_types = COUNT(DISTINCT service_request_type). The outer query then ranks with RANK() OVER (ORDER BY total_reqs DESC) and buckets the volume with a CASE ladder from 'critical' down to 'light'.

WITH dept_totals AS (SELECT department, COUNT(*) AS total_reqs, COUNT(DISTINCT service_request_type) AS distinct_types FROM requests GROUP BY department) SELECT department, total_reqs, distinct_types, RANK() OVER (ORDER BY total_reqs DESC) AS load_rank, CASE WHEN total_reqs >= 60000 THEN 'critical' WHEN total_reqs >= 30000 THEN 'heavy' WHEN total_reqs >= 5000 THEN 'moderate' ELSE 'light' END AS load_tier FROM dept_totals ORDER BY total_reqs DESC, department.

```sql
WITH dept_totals AS (SELECT department, COUNT(*) AS total_reqs, COUNT(DISTINCT service_request_type) AS distinct_types FROM requests GROUP BY department) SELECT department, total_reqs, distinct_types, RANK() OVER (ORDER BY total_reqs DESC) AS load_rank, CASE WHEN total_reqs >= 60000 THEN 'critical' WHEN total_reqs >= 30000 THEN 'heavy' WHEN total_reqs >= 5000 THEN 'moderate' ELSE 'light' END AS load_tier FROM dept_totals ORDER BY total_reqs DESC, department
```

exercises.prompt: Dispatch wants a Pareto view of where requests come from. Among requests with a non-null neighborhood, aggregate to one row per neighborhood with reqs = COUNT(*). Return the top 15 neighborhoods by volume with: neighborhood, reqs, pct_of_total = ROUND(100.0 * reqs / SUM(reqs) OVER (), 2) (share of all non-null-neighborhood requests), cumulative_pct = ROUND(SUM(reqs) OVER (ORDER BY reqs DESC, neighborhood ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) * 100.0 / SUM(reqs) OVER (), 2) (running share down the ranking), and hotspot_rank = ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY reqs DESC, neighborhood). Order by reqs descending, then neighborhood ascending, and keep only the first 15 rows.
exercises.referenceSql: WITH hood AS (SELECT neighborhood, COUNT(*) AS reqs FROM requests WHERE neighborhood IS NOT NULL GROUP BY neighborhood) SELECT neighborhood, reqs, ROUND(100.0 * reqs / SUM(reqs) OVER (), 2) AS pct_of_total, ROUND(SUM(reqs) OVER (ORDER BY reqs DESC, neighborhood ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) * 100.0 / SUM(reqs) OVER (), 2) AS cumulative_pct, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY reqs DESC, neighborhood) AS hotspot_rank FROM hood ORDER BY reqs DESC, neighborhood LIMIT 15
hints: Aggregate to one row per neighborhood in a hood CTE (non-null neighborhoods only). Then three windows do the work over the full set before the LIMIT: SUM(reqs) OVER () is the grand-total denominator for pct_of_total; SUM(reqs) OVER (ORDER BY reqs DESC, neighborhood ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) is the running total that, divided by that same grand total, gives cumulative_pct; and ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY reqs DESC, neighborhood) is the hotspot_rank. Order and LIMIT 15 last.

WITH hood AS (SELECT neighborhood, COUNT(*) AS reqs FROM requests WHERE neighborhood IS NOT NULL GROUP BY neighborhood) SELECT neighborhood, reqs, ROUND(100.0 * reqs / SUM(reqs) OVER (), 2) AS pct_of_total, ROUND(SUM(reqs) OVER (ORDER BY reqs DESC, neighborhood ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) * 100.0 / SUM(reqs) OVER (), 2) AS cumulative_pct, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY reqs DESC, neighborhood) AS hotspot_rank FROM hood ORDER BY reqs DESC, neighborhood LIMIT 15.

```sql
WITH hood AS (SELECT neighborhood, COUNT(*) AS reqs FROM requests WHERE neighborhood IS NOT NULL GROUP BY neighborhood) SELECT neighborhood, reqs, ROUND(100.0 * reqs / SUM(reqs) OVER (), 2) AS pct_of_total, ROUND(SUM(reqs) OVER (ORDER BY reqs DESC, neighborhood ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) * 100.0 / SUM(reqs) OVER (), 2) AS cumulative_pct, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY reqs DESC, neighborhood) AS hotspot_rank FROM hood ORDER BY reqs DESC, neighborhood LIMIT 15
```

exercises.prompt: Build dispatch's weekly briefing. Aggregate requests into one row per week with wk = CAST(DATE_TRUNC('week', created_date) AS DATE) and reqs = COUNT(*), over the full dataset. Then attach two windowed measures across the weekly series: prev_reqs = LAG(reqs) over weeks ordered ascending, and rolling_4w = ROUND(AVG(reqs) OVER (ORDER BY wk ROWS BETWEEN 3 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW), 1) (this week and the three before it). Finally keep weeks from 2025-09-01 through 2026-06-30 and return wk, reqs, prev_reqs, rolling_4w, wow_pct = ROUND(100.0 * (reqs - prev_reqs) / prev_reqs, 1), and status (CASE: 'above trend' when reqs are greater than rolling_4w, otherwise 'at or below trend'). Order by wk ascending. Compute the windows over the whole weekly series in a CTE before filtering to the display range.
exercises.referenceSql: WITH weekly AS (SELECT CAST(DATE_TRUNC('week', created_date) AS DATE) AS wk, COUNT(*) AS reqs FROM requests GROUP BY wk), enriched AS (SELECT wk, reqs, LAG(reqs) OVER (ORDER BY wk) AS prev_reqs, ROUND(AVG(reqs) OVER (ORDER BY wk ROWS BETWEEN 3 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW), 1) AS rolling_4w FROM weekly) SELECT wk, reqs, prev_reqs, rolling_4w, ROUND(100.0 * (reqs - prev_reqs) / prev_reqs, 1) AS wow_pct, CASE WHEN reqs > rolling_4w THEN 'above trend' ELSE 'at or below trend' END AS status FROM enriched WHERE wk >= DATE '2025-09-01' AND wk <= DATE '2026-06-30' ORDER BY wk
hints: Two windows ride on the same weekly series, so build it once and filter last. A weekly CTE groups requests by DATE_TRUNC('week', created_date) cast to DATE. An enriched CTE adds LAG(reqs) OVER (ORDER BY wk) for prev_reqs and AVG(reqs) OVER (ORDER BY wk ROWS BETWEEN 3 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) for the rolling 4-week average — both over every week, so the earliest shown week still has neighbors behind it. The outer query filters to the display range, computes wow_pct, and flags status with CASE.

WITH weekly AS (SELECT CAST(DATE_TRUNC('week', created_date) AS DATE) AS wk, COUNT(*) AS reqs FROM requests GROUP BY wk), enriched AS (SELECT wk, reqs, LAG(reqs) OVER (ORDER BY wk) AS prev_reqs, ROUND(AVG(reqs) OVER (ORDER BY wk ROWS BETWEEN 3 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW), 1) AS rolling_4w FROM weekly) SELECT wk, reqs, prev_reqs, rolling_4w, ROUND(100.0 * (reqs - prev_reqs) / prev_reqs, 1) AS wow_pct, CASE WHEN reqs > rolling_4w THEN 'above trend' ELSE 'at or below trend' END AS status FROM enriched WHERE wk >= DATE '2025-09-01' AND wk <= DATE '2026-06-30' ORDER BY wk.

```sql
WITH weekly AS (SELECT CAST(DATE_TRUNC('week', created_date) AS DATE) AS wk, COUNT(*) AS reqs FROM requests GROUP BY wk), enriched AS (SELECT wk, reqs, LAG(reqs) OVER (ORDER BY wk) AS prev_reqs, ROUND(AVG(reqs) OVER (ORDER BY wk ROWS BETWEEN 3 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW), 1) AS rolling_4w FROM weekly) SELECT wk, reqs, prev_reqs, rolling_4w, ROUND(100.0 * (reqs - prev_reqs) / prev_reqs, 1) AS wow_pct, CASE WHEN reqs > rolling_4w THEN 'above trend' ELSE 'at or below trend' END AS status FROM enriched WHERE wk >= DATE '2025-09-01' AND wk <= DATE '2026-06-30' ORDER BY wk
```
